Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Donald Davidson

1917 – 2003

Anomalous monism: mental events are physical events, but mental descriptions are not law-governed.

Donald Davidson’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a unified position in which ontology, action theory, philosophy of psychology, semantics, and epistemology are inseparable. His central view is not merely that mental events are physical events. It is that mindedness is constituted by patterns of rationally interpretable attitudes and actions, that these patterns are embedded in language and social interaction, and that they are instantiated in the physical world without being reducible to the vocabulary or laws of physics.

A compact formula for the position would be: ontological monism, explanatory pluralism, rational holism, semantic externalism, and anti-Cartesian epistemology. Davidson gives us a physicalist theory of mind without reductionism, a causal theory of action without behaviorism, and an account of psychological explanation that preserves the autonomy of the human sciences while rejecting any metaphysical dualism between mind and nature.

His place in twentieth-century philosophy of mind is therefore pivotal. He enters after the decline of behaviorism and during the rise of identity theory, functionalism, and nonreductive physicalism. His work helps define one of the major options in post-Cartesian philosophy of mind: a view on which the mental is real, causally efficacious, physically instantiated, and yet not capturable by strict psychophysical laws. Davidson’s work is influential across action theory, philosophy of language, metaphysics, epistemology, and mind; commentators regularly treat his accounts of anomalous monism, radical interpretation, and first-person authority as parts of one philosophical project rather than isolated doctrines.

1. Historical Position: What Davidson Was Responding To

Davidson’s philosophy of mind emerges in a field dominated by several competing strategies for naturalizing mind.

The first was behaviorism, especially logical and methodological behaviorism. Behaviorism attempted, in different ways, to tie mental-state attribution to behavioral dispositions or to the methods of behavioral science. Its attraction was anti-Cartesian: it rejected private inner objects as the foundation of psychology. Its weakness was that it seemed unable to account for the inner complexity, normativity, and semantic content of belief, desire, intention, and reasoning.

The second was type identity theory, according to which mental state types are identical with brain state types. Pain, belief, desire, and other mental states were to be identified with neurophysiological kinds, much as lightning is identified with electrical discharge. This promised a robust physicalism, but it faced difficulties from multiple realizability: the same mental kind might be realized in different neural, biological, or even artificial systems.

The third was functionalism, which became especially prominent in the last third of the twentieth century. Functionalism analyzes mental states by their causal roles: their relations to inputs, outputs, and other mental states, rather than by their intrinsic physical constitution. It was more flexible than type identity theory and better suited to multiple realizability, but Davidson’s position differs from it in an important respect. Davidson does not primarily define mental states by functional role in a quasi-computational causal system. He defines the space of the mental through interpretation, rationality, intentional content, and the constitutive norms governing the attribution of belief and desire.

Davidson’s mature view also anticipates and contributes to externalism in philosophy of mind. Externalism denies that mental content is fixed wholly by what is inside the head or body; Davidson’s own version ties thought and meaning to causal interaction with the world and to social triangulation among speaker, interpreter, and shared objects.

Against this background, Davidson’s position is distinctive. He rejects Cartesian dualism, but also rejects reductive materialism. He rejects behaviorism, but insists that the evidence for mental attribution is public, behavioral, linguistic, and worldly. He accepts that mental events are physical events, but denies that psychology can be reduced to strict psychophysical laws. He treats reasons as causes, but also treats reason explanation as irreducibly normative.

2. The Core: Mind as Rationally Interpretable Agency

Davidson’s philosophy of mind begins with action. His 1963 essay “Actions, Reasons, and Causes” is a decisive intervention in twentieth-century action theory. At the time, many philosophers influenced by ordinary-language philosophy and Wittgensteinian themes held that explaining an action by giving an agent’s reason was not a causal explanation. To say that someone turned on the light because he wanted to illuminate the room and believed flipping the switch would do so was, on this anti-causal view, to make the action intelligible, not to identify a cause.

Davidson rejects that separation. His famous thesis is that rationalization is a species of causal explanation. A reason explains an action only if it is the reason for which the agent acted, and that “because” is causal. He calls the explanatory reason a primary reason, consisting of a pro-attitude and a relevant belief: roughly, wanting, valuing, desiring, approving, or being disposed toward some end, together with the belief that the action will promote or realize that end.

This is the foundation of Davidson’s philosophy of psychology. A person’s action is not merely bodily motion. It is bodily motion under a description. One and the same event may be described as moving one’s finger, flipping a switch, turning on a light, alerting a prowler, or causing a disturbance. The action may be intentional under one description and not under another. Davidson’s own examples make this point central: the agent may intentionally flip the switch and turn on the light, while unintentionally alerting the prowler. The ontology is one event; the intentionality depends on description.

This “action under a description” thesis becomes a general metaphysical tool. Mental explanation is description-sensitive. Causation itself relates events, but whether an event is explained as a reason, a bodily movement, a neural event, or a physical process depends on the conceptual scheme in which it is described. Davidson thereby combines realism about causes with pluralism about explanations.

For Davidson, reasons do two things at once. They rationalize and they cause. They rationalize because they display the action as intelligible from the agent’s point of view; they show what there was, for that agent, to be said for doing it. They cause because not every reason an agent has is the reason for which the agent acts. Someone may have a reason to exercise, possess the relevant desire and belief, and yet go running for an entirely different reason, such as vanity or fear. To distinguish a reason one merely has from the reason for which one acted, Davidson argues, we need a causal relation.

The consequence is profound: psychology, at least the psychology of action, is both interpretive and causal. It is interpretive because it explains by placing action in a rational pattern of attitudes. It is causal because the attitudes that explain are among the causes of the action. Davidson thereby opposes both anti-causal hermeneutics and reductionist physicalism.

3. Events, Descriptions, and the Ontology of Mind

Davidson’s metaphysics of mind depends on his ontology of events. Events are concrete, dated particulars. A mental event, such as a judging, deciding, noticing, intending, or wanting, is not a ghostly occurrence outside nature. It is an event that can be described in mental vocabulary. The same event may also be describable physically. Davidson’s theory is explicitly concerned with individual events, rather than with abstract types, properties, or repeatable universals.

This lets Davidson separate three questions that earlier debates often ran together.

First, are mental events real? Yes. Believings, intendings, judgings, and decidings are real events.

Second, are they physical? Davidson says yes, at least for mental events that enter into causal relations with physical events. This is his monism.

Third, are mental kinds reducible to physical kinds? Davidson says no. This is his anomalism.

The distinction between event identity and description is the key. A single event can be picked out by many true descriptions. Suppose someone intentionally raises an arm, thereby knocking over a glass, contracting certain muscles, firing certain neurons, and changing the distribution of mass in space. These may all describe one complex event or overlapping events at different levels. The fact that one description is mental and another physical does not imply two entities. It implies one entity under different descriptions.

Davidson also uses intentionality to distinguish the mental. A mental description is one that uses intentional idioms: belief that, desire that, intention to, perception that, meaning that, and so on. Davidson is not primarily interested in defining the mental through privacy, immateriality, or introspective givenness. The mark of the mental, in his central work, is intentional content.

This emphasis matters. Davidson’s philosophy of mind is much stronger as a theory of propositional attitudes and intentional action than as a theory of raw sensation or phenomenal consciousness. He is interested above all in belief, desire, intention, meaning, agency, interpretation, rationality, and self-knowledge. He has things to say about sensations, but the architecture of the system is built around intentional psychology.

4. Anomalous Monism: The Central Doctrine

Davidson’s best-known doctrine is anomalous monism, introduced in “Mental Events.” It is “monism” because mental events are identical with physical events. It is “anomalous” because there are no strict laws connecting mental and physical predicates, and no strict laws of psychology in the sense in which physics seeks strict laws. Davidson presents the view as a way of reconciling three commitments: mental events interact causally with physical events; causality requires laws; and the mental is not governed by strict laws.

The three principles are these.

The first is the principle of causal interaction: at least some mental events causally interact with physical events. For example, a desire and belief may cause a bodily movement; a perceptual stimulation may cause a belief.

The second is the principle of the nomological character of causality: where there is causality, there must be a strict law covering the events, at least under some description.

The third is the principle of the anomalism of the mental: there are no strict deterministic laws on the basis of which mental events can be predicted and explained as mental events.

At first, these principles seem inconsistent. If mental events cause physical events, and causal relations require laws, then mental-physical causal relations should fall under strict laws. But Davidson denies strict psychophysical laws. His solution is that causal relations are extensional relations between events, while laws hold under descriptions. A mental event that causes a physical event must fall under some strict law, but not under a mental description. It falls under a physical description. Therefore the mental event has a physical description; therefore it is a physical event.

The argument can be reconstructed as follows.

Let M be a mental event, such as an intention, and P a physical event, such as a bodily movement. Suppose M causes P. If M causes P, then by the nomological principle there are descriptions of M and P under which they instantiate a strict law. But, by the anomalism of the mental, no strict law connects mental descriptions as such with physical descriptions as such. Therefore the law that covers the causal relation must describe both events physically. If M can be described physically, then M is a physical event. Thus, causally efficacious mental events are identical with physical events.

The result is not type identity. Davidson does not say that every belief of a certain kind is identical with one neurophysiological type. Nor does he say that psychological predicates can be translated into physical predicates. His view is a token-identity theory: individual mental events are individual physical events, but mental kinds are not reducible to physical kinds.

This doctrine gives Davidson a distinctive form of nonreductive physicalism. It is physicalist because the events are physical. It is nonreductive because the conceptual and explanatory scheme of the mental is not reducible to physics. It is realist because mental events are genuine causes. It is anti-dualist because those events are not nonphysical substances or ghostly episodes. It is anti-eliminativist because psychological explanation remains indispensable.

5. Why the Mental Is “Anomalous”

The word “anomalous” does not mean chaotic, unreal, or inexplicable. Davidson does not deny that psychology can contain useful generalizations. He denies that there are strict laws formulated in mental vocabulary or strict bridge laws linking mental and physical vocabularies.

The reason is not ignorance. It is not that psychology is presently immature and may eventually become physics. Davidson’s claim is conceptual. Mental predicates are governed by norms of rationality, coherence, consistency, and intelligibility. Physical predicates are governed by different constitutive standards: measurement, causal regularity, spatial-temporal relations, and quantitative law. To attribute beliefs and desires to someone is not merely to correlate inner states with behavior. It is to interpret the person as, by and large, a rational agent whose attitudes make sense together and whose beliefs are largely responsive to the world.

This is Davidson’s deepest reason for anti-reductionism. Mental attribution is holistic. Whether someone believes that it will rain depends not only on one internal state but on a wider network of beliefs, desires, utterances, perceptions, inferential dispositions, and worldly circumstances. If an agent’s utterances and behavior appear irrational, interpretation does not immediately assign bizarre beliefs. It adjusts the total pattern: perhaps the agent uses words differently, lacks information, is joking, is deceived, is confused, or has other beliefs we have not yet identified. The mental is therefore fixed by a large pattern of rational accommodation.

This is why strict psychophysical laws are impossible for Davidson. A strict law requires stable predicates whose application is independent of such holistic rational adjustment. But mental predicates are not like that. Their application depends on how they fit into an interpretive scheme. If we tried to make belief-attribution strictly lawlike by tying it mechanically to neural states or behavioral outputs, we would lose the normative, contentful character that makes it belief-attribution in the first place.

Davidson’s point is not that minds are outside nature. It is that the conceptual scheme of agency is not the conceptual scheme of physics. Physics explains events by subsuming them under strict laws. Psychology explains actions by showing how they make sense in light of the agent’s beliefs, desires, and intentions. These explanations can both be true of the same physical world.

6. Philosophy of Psychology: A Davidsonian Model

Davidson’s philosophy of psychology can be stated as follows: psychology is a causal, interpretive, non-strict, rationalizing discipline whose subject matter is physically embodied agency.

This view has several components.

First, psychological explanation is causal. Beliefs, desires, intentions, hopes, fears, and decisions can cause actions and other mental events. Davidson does not reduce them to neural events in explanatory practice, but he does insist that they are genuine causal relata.

Second, psychological explanation is rationalizing. To explain someone’s action psychologically is not merely to identify a preceding internal trigger. It is to show why the action was intelligible to the agent. This is why the primary explanatory form is: the agent wanted or valued something, believed that acting in a certain way would promote it, and acted because of that reason.

Third, psychology is holistic. A single belief cannot be identified independently of a network of other beliefs, desires, linguistic dispositions, and worldly relations. Davidson inherits from Quine a suspicion of atomistic meanings and isolated mental contents, but transforms it into a theory of interpretation and agency. Radical interpretation cannot assign meanings without beliefs, or beliefs without meanings; the two must be determined together by making the speaker intelligible as largely coherent and largely right about the shared world.

Fourth, psychology is normative. This does not mean moralistic. It means that the constitutive standards of belief and action involve rational norms: consistency, coherence, evidential responsiveness, means-end intelligibility, and truth. To attribute beliefs is to place the subject under these standards. A creature whose states were not interpretable at all in relation to such standards would not count as having beliefs and desires in the full Davidsonian sense.

Fifth, psychology is not reducible to neuroscience, even if every psychological event is physically realized. Neuroscience may explain the same event under neural descriptions. It may identify mechanisms that enable perception, memory, and action. But a neural explanation of why a bodily movement occurred is not automatically an explanation of why the agent intentionally did what he did. The latter requires reasons, descriptions, and interpretation.

This gives Davidson a middle path between two extremes. Against reductive physicalism, he insists that the language of reasons is not replaceable by physics. Against dualism and anti-naturalist hermeneutics, he insists that reasons are causes and that mental events are part of the physical order.

7. Mental Causation and the Threat of Epiphenomenalism

Davidson’s solution to the mind-body problem has generated a major objection: Does anomalous monism make mental properties causally irrelevant?

The worry is this. Davidson says mental events are physical events, and that causal relations fall under strict laws only when the events are physically described. But if the mental description plays no role in the strict causal law, then it may seem that the event causes what it causes only in virtue of its physical properties, not in virtue of its mental properties. The mental, critics argue, becomes epiphenomenal: present, perhaps explanatory in some loose sense, but not genuinely causally efficacious.

Davidson’s response is to distinguish causation from causal explanation. Causation is a relation between particular events. Events cause events. Properties, descriptions, or predicates do not literally cause anything. A mental event can be causally efficacious because it is the very event that causes the later event. Whether we explain it mentally or physically is a matter of description. Mental descriptions are indispensable because they rationalize the action; physical descriptions may place the same events under strict laws.

For Davidson, then, the demand that mental properties themselves do causal work is misplaced. The event does the causal work. The description determines the explanatory relevance. If my desire for coffee and my belief that there is coffee in the kitchen explain why I walk to the kitchen, those mental descriptions explain the action by displaying it as rational. The very event so described may also be a neurophysiological event covered by physical laws. There are not two causes, one mental and one physical. There is one event under different explanatory schemes.

Whether this fully answers the epiphenomenalism objection remains contested. Many later philosophers of mind think Davidson saves the causal efficacy of mental events but not clearly the causal relevance of mental properties. This debate became central in later discussions of nonreductive physicalism, exclusion arguments, and the causal closure of the physical. Davidson’s position is therefore historically important not only because it offered a solution, but because it helped define the problem that later philosophers had to confront.

8. Radical Interpretation: Mind, Meaning, and Rationality

Davidson’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his philosophy of language. His account of radical interpretation is modeled partly in contrast with Quine’s radical translation. The radical interpreter tries to understand a speaker from scratch, assigning meanings to utterances and beliefs to the speaker in a way that makes best sense of the speaker’s behavior in the world.

The central difficulty is that meaning and belief are interdependent. To know what a speaker means by “snow is white,” we need to know what the speaker believes. But to know what the speaker believes, we need to know what the speaker’s words mean. Davidson’s solution is the principle of charity: interpretation must attribute to the speaker, by and large, true beliefs and rational connections among beliefs, desires, and utterances.

This is not merely a practical recommendation. For Davidson, charity is constitutive of interpretation. A being whose utterances and actions could not be made largely intelligible as rational and world-directed would not be interpretable as a thinker with propositional attitudes. Interpretation does not discover a fully private mental realm hidden behind behavior; it constitutes the conditions under which mental content can be assigned at all.

Radical interpretation also supports Davidson’s holism. We do not assign one belief at a time, independently. We assign an entire pattern of beliefs, desires, meanings, perceptions, and actions. Content is determined by its place in this pattern and by the subject’s causal relations to the world.

This has an important consequence for psychology. A Davidsonian psychology is not an inner-object theory. Beliefs are not private mental items whose contents are fixed independently of interpretation. Nor are they mere behavioral dispositions. They are states of agents whose identity depends on rational, linguistic, and worldly relations.

9. Externalism and Triangulation

Davidson’s later philosophy develops a strong externalist account of thought. A thinker’s mental contents are not fixed solely by internal states. They depend on causal relations to objects and events in the world, and on social relations to other interpreters.

The key idea is triangulation. Thought requires a triangular relation among at least two creatures and a shared world. One creature responds to an object; another creature responds both to that object and to the first creature’s response. Through this shared pattern, the object of thought becomes determinate. The common cause in the world helps fix what the creatures are responding to, talking about, and thinking about. Davidson uses this framework to connect objectivity, communication, and thought.

This is also the basis of Davidson’s controversial thesis that propositional thought requires language. In “Rational Animals,” he argues that a creature cannot have full propositional attitudes unless it has the concept of belief, and the concept of belief requires the contrast between what is believed and what is objectively true. That contrast, he argues, emerges only in linguistic communication. A creature may discriminate, learn, remember, expect, and respond intelligently to its environment, but full belief requires grasping the possibility of error: the difference between how things seem and how they are.

Davidson’s claim is not that every thought must be linguistically formulated as an inner sentence. Nor is it that nonlinguistic animals are mere machines. His claim is stricter and more technical: possession of propositional attitudes, in the full sense relevant to belief, desire, and rational interpretation, requires the conceptual resources that arise only in a linguistic, intersubjective setting. A creature must be interpretable as a participant in a shared world of objective truth.

This is one of Davidson’s most anti-Cartesian moves. Descartes begins with the solitary thinker and asks how that thinker can know the external world and other minds. Davidson begins with interpretation, communication, and shared causes in a common world. On his view, mind is not first private and then later connected to the world. Mind is world-involving and socially triangulated from the start.

10. First-Person Authority Without Cartesian Infallibility

Davidson also offers an account of self-knowledge. He accepts that people normally have a special authority concerning their own beliefs, desires, intentions, and meanings. If a speaker sincerely says, “I believe it will rain,” we ordinarily presume that the speaker knows what he believes. But Davidson denies that this authority rests on introspection understood as inner perception. He also denies that self-knowledge is infallible, incorrigible, or based on private mental objects.

The problem is subtle. If self-knowledge is not based on evidence, and not based on inner observation, why does first-person authority exist? Davidson’s answer again comes from interpretation. A speaker’s authority over his own attitudes is built into the conditions of interpreting speech. In general, an interpreter must assume that a speaker understands what he means and knows what he holds true. If the speaker did not normally know what his own utterances meant, interpretation itself would collapse.

This does not make the speaker immune to error. A person can be self-deceived, confused, insincere, forgetful, or ignorant of some of his motives. Evidence available to others may override a self-ascription. But the first-person case has a distinctive default authority because avowals of belief and intention are not normally reports based on evidence. They are direct expressions of the subject’s rational position.

Davidson later places this in a broader epistemological triad: knowledge of one’s own mind, knowledge of other minds, and knowledge of the external world are not three independent achievements. They are mutually dependent aspects of a single interpretive situation. One knows one’s own mind only as a speaker and agent in a public world; one knows others through their behavior and speech; one knows the world as the shared object of interpretation and action.

This dissolves, rather than solves in traditional form, the Cartesian problem. There is no isolated inner foundation from which world and others must be reconstructed. The very possibility of thought presupposes a world shared with others.

11. Irrationality, Akrasia, and the Divided Mind

Davidson’s emphasis on rationality might seem to make irrationality impossible. If mental attribution requires rational interpretation, how can there be weakness of will, self-deception, wishful thinking, or inconsistency?

Davidson’s answer is that irrationality is intelligible only against a background of rationality. A wholly irrational creature would not be interpretable as a thinker. But local irrationality can occur within a largely rational system. In his work on weakness of will and self-deception, Davidson argues that failures of rational integration can be understood by seeing the mind as containing semi-independent structures or compartments. The irrational agent need not explicitly accept a contradiction in one fully unified act of judgment; rather, conflicting attitudes may be held apart in ways that prevent the normal pressure toward consistency.

This is important for Davidson’s philosophy of psychology. It allows him to preserve the constitutive role of rationality without idealizing human beings as perfectly rational. Interpretation requires enough rationality to identify beliefs and desires, but it does not require perfect coherence. Indeed, many psychological phenomena become interesting precisely because they are deviations from rational integration within a system that remains intelligible overall.

Davidson’s view here has affinities with psychoanalytic explanation, though he does not simply adopt Freud’s metapsychology. Self-deception and akrasia require a structure in which one part of the mind can operate with some independence from another. But these parts are not literal homunculi. They are patterns of attitudes and dispositions whose partial isolation explains failures of rational unity.

12. Davidson Against Cartesianism

Davidson’s philosophy of mind is anti-Cartesian in at least five ways.

First, he rejects substance dualism. There are no nonphysical mental substances standing outside nature. Mental events are physical events.

Second, he rejects the idea that the mental is essentially private. Mental content depends on public language, observable behavior, shared causes, and interpretive relations.

Third, he rejects introspection as the foundation of self-knowledge. First-person authority is real, but it is not based on inner perception of private objects.

Fourth, he rejects the epistemological priority of the first person. Knowledge of self, other minds, and world are interdependent.

Fifth, he rejects the idea of a private conceptual scheme sealed off from the world. His externalism and triangulation make thought dependent on causal and social relations to a shared environment.

This anti-Cartesianism distinguishes Davidson from both dualist and empiricist traditions. He does not replace the Cartesian inner theater with behaviorist surfaces. Rather, he reconceives mind as public, interpretive, embodied, and world-involving while still preserving the reality of belief, desire, intention, and self-knowledge.

13. Davidson and the Autonomy of the Human Sciences

Davidson’s view has direct implications for the philosophy of psychology and the human sciences.

A strict physical science seeks exceptionless laws stated in a vocabulary whose predicates are not constitutively governed by norms of rationality. Human psychology, by contrast, works with concepts like belief, intention, shame, hope, resentment, decision, and interpretation. These concepts are not merely labels for inner physical kinds. They belong to a normative framework in which agents are understood as acting for reasons.

This does not make psychology unscientific. It means that psychology is not physics. It may use experiments, causal models, statistics, and empirical generalizations. But where it concerns propositional attitudes and intentional action, it cannot eliminate the interpretive norms that make its subject matter mental. Davidson therefore gives philosophical grounding to the autonomy of ordinary psychological explanation and, by extension, parts of the human sciences.

His view also explains why predictive psychology is limited. Human beings are interpretable under rational norms, but rational norms do not generate strict predictions. To know someone’s beliefs and desires is often to understand what would make sense for them to do, not to calculate with physical necessity what they must do. The mental order is intelligible, but not lawlike in the strict sense.

This is one reason Davidson is important for debates between naturalism and hermeneutics. He is a naturalist about ontology and causation, but not a reductionist about explanation. His philosophy of psychology is therefore a form of naturalized interpretivism.

14. The Extracted Davidsonian Philosophy of Mind

A comprehensive Davidsonian philosophy of mind can be summarized in the following theses.

1. Event monism. There is one world of events. Mental events are not ontologically separate from physical events. When mental events cause or are caused by physical events, they are identical with physical events.

2. Token physicalism without type reduction. Individual mental events are physical, but mental kinds are not reducible to physical kinds. There are no strict psychophysical laws pairing mental types with physical types.

3. The mental is marked by intentionality. Davidson’s central mental phenomena are propositional attitudes: belief, desire, intention, judgment, perception-that, meaning, and related states. The philosophy of mind is therefore centered on contentful agency rather than on raw qualitative consciousness.

4. Reasons are causes. The explanation of intentional action by reasons is a genuine causal explanation. But it is causal in a special way: the cause is identified under a description that rationalizes the action.

5. Actions are events under descriptions. The same physical occurrence may be intentional under one description and unintentional under another. This description-relativity is essential to action theory and to psychology.

6. Psychological explanation is rationalizing explanation. To explain an action psychologically is to locate it in a pattern of beliefs, desires, intentions, and values that makes it intelligible from the agent’s point of view.

7. The mental is anomalous. There are no strict laws of the mental and no strict bridge laws from the mental to the physical. The reason is that mental predicates are governed by holistic norms of rational interpretation.

8. Mental attribution is holistic. A belief or desire cannot be identified in isolation. Its content depends on its place within a network of other attitudes, utterances, actions, and worldly relations.

9. Interpretation is constitutive. The assignment of beliefs and meanings depends on the principle of charity: we must interpret speakers as largely rational and largely right about the world. This is not optional kindness but a condition of making sense of them as thinkers.

10. Content is externalist. Thought depends on causal relations to the world and on social triangulation among persons and shared objects. Meaning and belief are not fixed wholly inside the head.

11. Language is central to full propositional thought. Davidson holds that belief requires the concept of objective truth, and that this concept emerges only in linguistic communication. This makes rationality, in the full propositional sense, a social achievement.

12. First-person authority is real but fallible. A person normally has special authority about his own beliefs and intentions, but not because he observes private mental objects. The authority is rooted in the conditions of interpretation and linguistic agency.

13. Irrationality is local, not global. Irrationality is possible only against a background of rationality. Akrasia and self-deception are failures of integration within a largely interpretable system.

14. Mind is not an inner realm. Mind is embodied in physical events, expressed in action, structured by rational norms, fixed through relations to the world, and sustained by social interpretation.

This is the complete Davidsonian picture: a person is a physically embodied agent whose actions and utterances are interpretable through a holistic network of rationally connected attitudes, whose contents are fixed by interaction with others and the world, and whose mental events are causally efficacious without being reducible to strict psychophysical laws.

15. Main Strengths of Davidson’s Position

Davidson’s philosophy of mind has several enduring strengths.

It preserves the reality of mental causation without dualism. Unlike epiphenomenalism, it does not treat beliefs and desires as idle by-products. Unlike substance dualism, it does not multiply entities beyond the physical world.

It explains why ordinary psychological explanation is indispensable. We do not understand action merely by tracking muscles, neurons, and stimuli. We understand agents by understanding what they take themselves to be doing and why.

It gives a powerful account of the normativity of mind. Belief is not just an internal registration; it is answerable to truth. Intention is not just a motor command; it is embedded in practical reasoning. Desire is not just impulse; it is interpretable in relation to values, beliefs, and action.

It avoids behaviorism without returning to Cartesian privacy. Davidson treats behavior, speech, and environment as evidence for interpretation, but he does not reduce mental states to behavior. The mental is public enough to be interpretable, but real enough to cause action.

It unifies philosophy of mind with philosophy of language. Meaning and belief are interdependent; interpretation assigns both together. This lets Davidson explain why thought is not merely internal representation but participation in a shared world of communication and truth.

It also anticipates later externalist and social approaches to mind. Davidson’s triangulation thesis makes sociality and world-involvement central, not peripheral, to thought.

16. Main Difficulties and Objections

The first major difficulty is the epiphenomenalism objection. Davidson’s claim that events, not properties, are causes is elegant, but many philosophers worry that it does not fully secure the causal relevance of mental properties. If the strict causal work is always done under physical descriptions, what exactly does the mental contribute causally? Davidson answers that mental descriptions give genuine causal explanations by rationalizing actions, but the debate remains unresolved.

The second difficulty concerns the principle that causation requires strict laws. Many later philosophers reject this principle or at least doubt that it fits actual scientific practice. If causal relations need not always be backed by strict deterministic laws, then Davidson’s formal route to anomalous monism becomes less secure. His conclusion may still be defensible, but the argument would need reconstruction.

The third difficulty is phenomenal consciousness. Davidson’s philosophy is far more developed as a theory of intentionality, action, belief, and interpretation than as a theory of qualia, pain, bodily sensation, or subjective experience. He acknowledges that his central discussion concerns propositional attitudes rather than sensations, but this leaves an important part of contemporary philosophy of mind under-theorized.

The fourth difficulty is the language requirement. Davidson’s claim that full propositional thought requires language is powerful but controversial. It seems to exclude infants and many nonhuman animals from having beliefs in the full sense. Davidson can allow that animals discriminate, learn, expect, and respond intelligently, but many philosophers and cognitive scientists think this is too restrictive.

The fifth difficulty is holism and indeterminacy. If belief and meaning are determined only by total patterns of interpretation, then individual mental-state attributions may seem less determinate than common sense assumes. Davidson accepts a degree of indeterminacy, but critics worry that this threatens psychological realism.

The sixth difficulty concerns cognitive science. Davidson’s anti-lawlike conception of the mental sits uneasily with computational, experimental, and neuroscientific approaches that seek systematic generalizations about cognition. A Davidsonian can respond that such sciences may study subpersonal mechanisms or statistically robust patterns without reducing the norm-governed space of reasons. But the relation between Davidsonian interpretation and empirical cognitive science remains complex.

17. Final Assessment

Davidson’s philosophy of mind is one of the most sophisticated forms of nonreductive naturalism in twentieth-century analytic philosophy. Its guiding thought is that the mental belongs wholly to the natural world, but not because it can be translated into physics. Rather, mental life is the life of agents whose behavior is interpretable in terms of reasons, whose thoughts are answerable to truth, whose contents are fixed in relation to a shared world, and whose self-knowledge depends on participation in linguistic and social practices.

The resulting view is neither Cartesian nor behaviorist, neither reductive nor dualist. It is a theory of the mind as physically embodied, causally efficacious, rationally structured, linguistically articulated, socially triangulated, and interpretively accessible.

Its historical importance lies in the way it reconfigured the mind-body problem. Davidson shifted attention from the question “Are mental states physical or nonphysical?” to the more subtle question “How can mental events be physical while mental explanation remains irreducibly rational and anomalous?” Much of later philosophy of mind, especially debates over mental causation, nonreductive physicalism, externalism, and the autonomy of psychology, develops in the space that Davidson helped define.

A Davidsonian philosophy of mind, extracted in its strongest form, says this: to have a mind is not to house a private inner theater, nor merely to instantiate neural machinery, but to be an embodied participant in a shared world whose actions and utterances can be made sense of through beliefs, desires, intentions, and reasons. The events are physical; the explanations are rational; the contents are worldly and social; the unity of mind is interpretive rather than mechanical.

PreviousU. T. Place & J. J. C. Smart NextHilary Putnam

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
